focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

Ten days later, Carmen was in the medication room doing the evening count when she heard it. At first it wasn't the words, just the cadence. Every nurse learned the difference between the TV room's daytime murmur and the local news anchor's emergency tone, the slight tightening in the broadcaster's delivery that meant something had happened nearby, something with a suspect, something with a mugshot.

She set down the clipboard and walked to the TV room.

It was mostly empty at 8:40 PM. Dinner was over, the last visitors had signed out at eight, and most of the Wing A residents were back in their rooms. Burt was dozing in the recliner nearest the screen, his mouth open, one hand curled around the armrest as if he were holding on to something in a dream. The volume was up too loud because nobody had adjusted it after Mrs. Petroski went back to her room, and Mrs. Petroski was functionally deaf.

The anchor described an arrest in Edison for prescription drug distribution. A man named DeShaun Briley, thirty-seven, had been picked up during a traffic stop with approximately four hundred tablets of oxycodone, eighty alprazolam, and an undetermined quantity of hydrocodone in the trunk of his vehicle. He was facing possession with intent to distribute and both state and federal charges. Bond had been set at seventy-five thousand.

Carmen didn't recognize the name. She didn't recognize the face in the mugshot, a heavy young man, scared-looking under the flat police lighting, his left eye slightly swollen as if he'd been rubbing it or hadn't slept. But she knew that number. Marcus's new buyer had been taking orders that size.

Her hand went to her badge and stayed there. The news anchor moved on to a car accident on Route 18.

She stood in the doorway another thirty...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hat weight and take nothing off the pile.

He put the handkerchief back in his pocket. The jacket hung on the hook. The envelope was in the pocket. Tomorrow: the post office form, the money order, the memo line, the outgoing mail. Next month: the same. The month after: the same. As long as the handkerchief stayed white.

He went back instead to one February in the split-level in Piscataway, him at the kitchen table after second shift trying to hide a wad of brown-streaked tissues under the ashtray. Maryann had lifted the ashtray, opened the top tissue, and looked at him until he picked up the phone. She didn't make a speech or start an argument. She just shoved Dr. Klein's number across on the grocery pad and waited. It had turned out to be bronchitis. The antibiotics cost two missed shifts, and Maryann pulled the copay out of the grocery envelope without saying what else that week would go without. If she were here now, the lie would've lasted until breakfast.

Instead he talked to her grave once a month and kept the important parts for himself: no ring, no cough, no fear. He told her about the grandkids and the small facts of their lives, and there was nobody there to push back.

Earl turned off the lamp. He lay back down and pulled the blanket to his chest and closed his eyes. The cough was still there — he could feel it waiting, a heaviness behind his sternum, not ready to surface but not gone either. It would come again. It came every night now. And every morning he would check, and the handkerchief would be white or it wouldn't, and the day he saw color on the cloth would be the day the clock he'd been ignoring started ticking loud enough for everyone to hear.

Not today.

He kept one hand over his sternum and counted off the breaths that would get him to morning.
